title: Add cutout aperture props
description: Add typed props for the new enclosure.cutoutaperture  element, supporting pill, rect, and circle aperture profiles with margin specifications and normalized dimensions in millimeters.
body:
Based on https://github.com/addibble/pcb-enclosure


## Summary

- add typed props for the new `<enclosure.cutoutaperture />` element
- expose the schema through `enclosureProps.cutoutaperture`
- use the lowercase enclosure convention for `enclosureProps.fdm.box`
- support `pill`, `rect`, and `circle` aperture profiles
- add shared `commonShapeProps`/`CommonShapeProps` discriminated unions where `pill` and `rect` require `width`/`height`, while `circle` requires `radius`
- extend each common shape schema branch with the aperture-specific optional `margin`
- normalize numeric and unit-string dimensions to millimeters
- export the schema and types and regenerate the package documentation

## Why

Components such as connectors need to carry their nominal enclosure-opening metadata alongside their footprint and CAD model. This additive props schema provides the typed JSX surface that downstream core and Circuit JSON support can serialize for enclosure generators.

The API follows the profile used by `addibble/pcb-enclosure`, the enclosure namespace established in #733, and the current `@tscircuit/props` distance parser and documentation conventions.

## Validation

- `bun test` (366 passing)
- `bun run typecheck`
- `bun run build`
- `bun run check-circular-deps`
- `bun run format:check`
- all required documentation generation scripts

title: Add namespaced enclosure props
description: Adds the initial enclosure.fdm.Box props schema and TypeScript interface, requiring a non-empty boardRef and supporting optional unit-aware dimensions.
body:
## Summary

- add the initial `enclosure.fdm.Box` props schema and TypeScript interface
- require a non-empty `boardRef`, allowing box dimensions to be inferred from the referenced board
- support optional unit-aware `width`, `height`, and `depth` overrides
- expose the schema through `enclosureProps.fdm.Box`
- document the generated props interface and add parsing, inference, and default tests

## Why

This establishes the props-side contract for namespaced JSX elements such as `<enclosure.fdm.Box />`. The schema remains React-independent so core can provide the runtime namespace wrapper separately.

## Developer impact

Consumers can import `EnclosureFdmBoxProps`, `enclosureFdmBoxProps`, or access the schema through `enclosureProps.fdm.Box`. `boardRef` is the only required sizing input; `width`, `height`, and `depth` are optional overrides. `wallThickness` defaults to 2 mm.

## Validation

- `bun test tests/enclosure-fdm-box.test.ts` (5 passing)
- `bunx tsc --noEmit`
- full props test suite previously run: 358 passing
- generated documentation scripts run

body:
## Summary

- aggregate PR authors, reviewers, and discussion participants by durable GitHub account ID
- persist `githubId`/`githubLogin` in weekly overview data and `contributorId` in PR analyses
- refresh cached PR identities from live GitHub payloads so regenerated data heals old logins
- correlate frontend history and sponsorship calculations by ID while keeping usernames as display fields
- retain a narrow legacy bridge for overview files written before IDs were stored; supplied IDs always win, so a reclaimed old username remains a different account
- use handbook-specific names such as `mergeContributorStatsByGitHubId`, `contributorIdentityKey`, and `getOrCreateContributorStats`, with identity reconciliation kept out of the entrypoint
- regenerate the July 2026 sponsorship CSV so `technologyet31-create` and `abdalraof-albarbar` are one contributor with weekly scores `[2, 1]`

## Root cause

Contributor and reviewer maps were keyed by mutable `user.login` values. The merged-PR processing also discarded GitHub's durable `user.id`, leaving cached analyses and weekly overview files unable to reconcile a later rename. Monthly sponsorship generation then treated the old and new logins as different people.

## Compatibility and migration

Weekly overview JSON remains username-keyed for compatibility, but each contributor record now carries its durable ID and most recently observed login. Runtime aggregation, cross-week history, PR grouping, and sponsorship grouping use the ID. The legacy mapping is consulted only when reading pre-ID files; once an ID is present it takes precedence over the login.

This is the durable-ID implementation of the rename problem also addressed by the manual alias approach in #344.

## Validation

- `bun run format:check`
- `bun test` — 40 passing
- `bunx tsc --noEmit`
- `bun run build`
- live GitHub GraphQL schema query confirming discussion authors expose `databaseId` through the `User` fragment
- regenerated `sponsorships/2026-07.csv` — one current-login row, total amount `$120`

title: Fix rotated-pad and duplicate DRC reports
description: Fixes false continuity errors and duplicate DRC reports by correctly identifying trace endpoints within rotated SMT pads and implementing a deduplication helper for DRC errors.
body:
## Summary

- use the real rotated copper-pad polygon when checking whether trace endpoints are inside rotated SMT pads and plated-hole rectangular pads
- add an exported DRC deduplication helper that prefers typed pad-trace and via-trace clearance errors over equivalent generic `pcb_trace_error` records
- apply deduplication to aggregate routing and all-check results without disabling any underlying collision check

## Root cause

`isPointInPad` did not support `rotated_pill_hole_with_rect_pad`, and its rotated SMT rectangle calculation treated degree values as radians. Valid trace endpoints inside those pads were therefore reported as disconnected.

Aggregate checks also combined the generic trace-overlap checker with the typed pad/via clearance checkers. The same physical trace/object pair could consequently be reported twice.

## Impact

The Dataset 18 analysis identified 112 false continuity errors and 898 duplicate records from these two causes. Replaying the affected continuity cases recognizes all 112 endpoints as contained by their related pads. Deduplication removes only generic records with a matching typed trace/object pair; trace-trace and unmatched errors remain unchanged.

## Validation

- `bun test` — 129 passed, 0 failed
- `bunx tsc --noEmit`
- `bun run build`
- end-to-end autorouter sample 3: continuity false positives 24 → 0 and duplicates 13 → 0
- end-to-end autorouter sample 16: 53 raw reports → 29 physical issues, with all 29 physical issues retained

title: Fix multiplicative missing-connection DRCs on branched source-trace networks
description: Fixes validation of PCB branch connections to prevent duplicate false errors on multi-port nets by ensuring port coverage is validated once per complete source trace.
body: ## Problem `checkTracesAreContiguous` validates every PCB branch against every expected port, producing duplicate false errors on large multi-port nets. ## Fix Validate port coverage once per complete source trace while considering all routed branches and endpoint-to-pad connections. Adds regression coverage for branches joined through a shared pad. ## Validation - 127 tests passing - SRJ18 repro reduced from 779 to 41 unique findings

title: Support selecting named simulation SVGs
description: Allows users to select an analog simulation by experiment name or Circuit JSON id, generating distinct URLs for each simulation on the homepage and preserving rendering of the first experiment when no selector is supplied.
body:
## Summary

- allow `sim` and `schsim` requests to select an analog simulation by experiment name or Circuit JSON id
- make the homepage discover pasted-circuit experiments and generate one `sim`/`schsim` URL pair per simulation
- preserve first-experiment rendering when no selector is supplied and fall back to ids for missing or duplicate names
- serialize calls to the shared ngspice engine so circuits with multiple experiments run reliably
- add real four-experiment ngspice coverage for root and group simulations and generated homepage URLs

## Why

Core can now produce multiple analog simulations in one circuit, but the SVG service previously rendered only the first experiment unless callers already knew its generated id. The homepage likewise emitted only one unqualified simulation URL pair. Named selection gives documentation and other consumers a stable way to request one static SVG for each experiment.

## User impact

Callers can pass `simulation_experiment_name` or `simulation_experiment_id` in the query string or POST body. Existing URLs continue to render the first simulation. On the homepage, users can paste a multi-simulation circuit and receive separately labeled graph and schematic-simulation URLs for every experiment.

## Validation

- `bun test tests/generate-urls.test.ts tests/multiple-simulation-svg.test.ts tests/schematic-simulation-svg.test.ts`
- manual Next dev-server flow: pasted the four-simulation fixture, generated eight named links, and opened the `Output Group` SVG
- `bunx tsc --noEmit`
- `bun run format:check`
- `bun run build`

title: Reduce Dataset 18 DRC issue count by 70.6% with exact Pipeline7 repair
description: Reduces DRC issue count in Dataset 18 by 70.6 through an exact repair process in Pipeline7, improving pass rates for multiple datasets.
body:
1.2% pass rate improvement to dataset 01
20% pass rate improvement to dataset 21
## Summary

- add a final Pipeline7 cleanup stage evaluated against the exact copper geometry emitted by the autorouter
- reuse the final Pipeline7 route conversion for both DRC evaluation and output
- run a bounded exact-repair baseline, then try a broader repulsion branch only while violations remain
- refine and select the broad branch only when its whole-board exact DRC count is strictly lower
- preserve already-clean routes instead of moving them through legacy post-solve relaxation
- pin `high-density-repair03` to the merged branch-portfolio implementation on `main` at `8b3eba6fb5edefd70d39210d88e38013d9096f4a`

## Why

Pipeline7's existing global repair stage does not evaluate exactly the same geometry emitted by final output. Dense Dataset 18 boards can therefore retain real trace/pad, trace/via, trace/trace, and via/via violations.

Repairing a single reported error per candidate also makes it difficult to improve dense failures within a practical iteration budget. This PR evaluates the final converted copper geometry, batches exact `pcb_trace_error` forces, and compares a conservative repair against an optional broader branch. A candidate is accepted only when the whole-board exact DRC snapshot improves.

## Dataset 18 benchmark

Paired full-run reported DRC issue counts across all 16 samples:

| Sample | Baseline | This PR | Reduction |
| --- | ---: | ---: | ---: |
| 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| 2 | 72 | 8 | 64 |
| 3 | 39 | 34 | 5 |
| 4 | 27 | 19 | 8 |
| 5 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| 6 | 506 | 99 | 407 |
| 7 | 28 | 4 | 24 |
| 8 | 124 | 31 | 93 |
| 9 | 41 | 24 | 17 |
| 10 | 12 | 3 | 9 |
| 11 | 27 | 2 | 25 |
| 12 | 192 | 12 | 180 |
| 13 | 336 | 113 | 223 |
| 14 | 283 | 149 | 134 |
| 15 | 327 | 94 | 233 |
| 16 | 39 | 11 | 28 |
| **Total** | **2,053** | **603** | **1,450 (70.6%)** |

- average reported issues per sample: `128.31 -> 37.69` (90.63 fewer per sample)
- 14 samples improved, 2 remained clean, and none regressed
- mean per-sample percentage reduction: 66.6% across the 14 nonzero baseline samples
- relaxed DRC pass rate remains 2/16 (12.5%); this PR substantially reduces remaining violations but does not claim additional fully clean samples

## Dependency

- tscircuit/high-density-repair03#25
- pinned merged-`main` commit: `8b3eba6fb5edefd70d39210d88e38013d9096f4a`
- the DRC-report deduplication prerequisite, #1642, is already merged into `main`

## Validation

- full 16-sample Dataset 18 Pipeline7 benchmark
- `bun run build`
- focused Pipeline7 integration and DRC regression tests
- changed snapshot set passes 9/9 in Linux-mode validation
- repair03: 25 tests passed and TypeScript typecheck passed
- the unrelated Pipeline8 `bugreport60-fe53a2` snapshot mismatch reproduces unchanged on the previous base

title: Choose lower-DRC exact repair branch in Pipeline7
description: Replaces the previous via-border-pressure experiment with an exact-DRC-gated repair portfolio, utilizing a normal 32-iteration exact cleanup as the baseline and implementing a broad repulsion strategy only when violations remain, ensuring that the final output is strictly better than the baseline.
body:
## Summary

- replace the previous via-border-pressure experiment with an exact-DRC-gated repair portfolio
- use the normal 32-iteration exact cleanup as the baseline
- try 3x broad repulsion only when violations remain, with an 8-iteration refinement cap
- keep the broad result only when its exact DRC count beats the completed baseline
- pin `high-density-repair03` to commit `78a5ee15baf1621a10bff115edceb59e94b982ed`
- update affected Pipeline7 snapshots and the existing DRC-count regression fixture

## Why

The previous PR3 heuristic changed via placement upstream but did not materially improve the real DRC result and regressed the benchmark pass count. Broad repulsion is high-leverage on dense failures, but it is unsafe when applied blindly.

This version measures both choices with Pipeline7's exact geometry evaluator. A broad candidate is not even refined unless it already has fewer exact violations than the completed baseline; its final output must still be strictly better. Clean inputs return unchanged.

## Measured impact

Paired exact-stage results on five saved srj18 failing cases:

| Sample | PR2 baseline | New portfolio |
| --- | ---: | ---: |
| 3 | 17 | 17 |
| 7 | 7 | 4 |
| 10 | 9 | 5 |
| 11 | 9 | 2 |
| 12 | 72 | 12 |
| **Total** | **114** | **40** |

That is a 64.9% reduction across these five targeted cases. This is not a claimed full-dataset pass-rate result.

Full Pipeline7 spot checks also produced:

- sample 5: remains clean (`1 → 0 → 0` through width/global/exact stages)
- sample 7: 4 remaining exact issues
- sample 10: 5 remaining exact issues
- sample 11: 2 remaining exact issues
- sample 12: `101 → 98 → 12`; exact stage 34s, full local run 195s
- built-in `bugreport-b5b3b9d8` DRC fixture: 6 issues → 2 trace issues, eliminating its via/trace and pad/trace errors

## Validation

- `bun run build`
- focused Pipeline7 integration and DRC regression tests
- changed snapshot set passes 9/9 in Linux-mode validation
- repair03: 25 tests passed and TypeScript typecheck passed

title: [41.6% reduction in DRC] Deduplicate autorouter DRC reports
description: Deduplicates autorouter DRC reports by applying a shared helper to eliminate duplicate records in the DRC results, improving the accuracy of error reporting.
body:
## Summary

- bump `@tscircuit/checks` to `^0.0.144`
- apply the shared `dedupePcbDrcErrors` helper to autorouter's assembled DRC result
- add a regression covering generic and typed pad/via reports for the same physical pairs

Depends on the merged checks fix: https://github.com/tscircuit/checks/pull/171

## Root cause

Autorouter's `getDrcErrors` calls the generic trace-overlap checker and the typed pad-trace/via-trace clearance checkers directly. It then concatenated all results, bypassing the aggregate APIs in `@tscircuit/checks` where equivalent generic records are deduplicated.

The shared helper retains the typed clearance record, including its actual and minimum clearance values, and removes only a generic `pcb_trace_error` with the same trace/object pair. Trace-trace and unmatched errors are unchanged.

## Impact

The Dataset 18 analysis found 898 duplicate records across the 14 failing samples. With the released `@tscircuit/checks@0.0.144` package and this integration, sample 3 reports 65 records for 65 physical issues, with its 13 duplicate records and 24 rotated-pad continuity false positives eliminated.

## Validation

- `bun test --timeout 9999999 tests/drc-deduplication.test.ts tests/drc-via-spacing.test.ts tests/utils/convertToCircuitJson-obstacle-connectivity-source-net-ids.test.ts` — 5 passed, 0 failed

- Dataset 18 sample 3 using published `@tscircuit/checks@0.0.144`: 65 raw reports, 65 physical issues, 0 continuity false positives, 0 duplicate records

title: Fix benchmark snapshot background zoom
description: Fixes the background alignment issue in benchmark snapshots by normalizing the SVG root background to the original viewBox coordinates, ensuring it zooms and pans correctly with the circuit geometry.
body:
## Summary

- normalize the generated SVG root background to the original `viewBox` coordinates
- keep the background aligned with circuit geometry during vector `viewBox` zoom and pan
- add regression coverage for non-zero, non-square `viewBox` bounds

## Root cause

PR #1631 changed snapshot zooming from a CSS transform to updates of the root SVG `viewBox` so zoomed circuits stay sharp. The generated background was still a percentage-sized root rectangle (`width="100%" height="100%"`), whose bounds resolve against the viewport rather than the circuit's original coordinate space. After zooming or panning, the circuit moved with the `viewBox` while the background could clip or remain at the old boundary.

This change converts that generated background rectangle to the original numeric `viewBox` bounds before embedding the SVG. It then behaves like the rest of the scene while preserving vector rendering.

## Validation

- `bun test tests/benchmark-snapshots-html.test.ts --timeout 9999999`
- `bunx tsc --noEmit`

- `git diff --check`

## What changed

- rename `HyperSingleIntraNodeSolver` to `PortfolioSingleIntraNodeSolver`
- rename the cached wrapper consistently
- update production imports, debugger types, diagnostics, and tests to use the portfolio terminology
- keep the previous class names and direct import paths as deprecated aliases

## Why

Follow-up to #1632. This solver coordinates a fitness-scheduled portfolio whose search breadth can expand dynamically. Calling it a `Hyper*` solver implied the more predictable/formal iteration allocation used by the hyperparameter supervisors elsewhere in the codebase.

`PortfolioSingleIntraNodeSolver` describes its responsibility without implying those iteration-allocation guarantees.

## Impact

- routing behavior and iteration allocation are unchanged
- solver/debug metadata now reports `PortfolioSingleIntraNodeSolver`
- existing consumers of `HyperSingleIntraNodeSolver` remain source-compatible through deprecated aliases

## Validation

- 15 focused tests pass
- production TypeScript and declaration build passes
- `bunx tsc --noEmit` passes
- Biome and diff checks pass

title: Expand high-density routing portfolio dynamically
description: Removes the fixed 8,000-round inner high-density cap and dynamically expands the routing portfolio based on candidate budgets, improving routing efficiency and accuracy.
body: ## What changed - remove Pipeline 7's fixed 8,000-round inner high-density cap - initialize candidates through their idempotent setup lifecycle so their problem- and effort-derived limits are known without advancing them - derive the supervisor limit from every live candidate's remaining work - preserve the existing ordering portfolio as the initial search - expand into the remaining deterministic ordering variants only after the initial portfolio consumes an aggregate exploration budget equal to its most expensive candidate's natural budget, or exhausts - leave candidate selection to the existing fitness scheduler - add a focused regression fixture and tests for the large sample 2 node ## Why The sample 2 node is routable at its original physical size. The fixed outer cap stopped the portfolio before a viable ordering completed; grow/shrink later returned invalid direct connections, producing long intersecting traces. An eager six-ordering portfolio removed those malformed traces, but changed winners on easy boards and regressed sample 5 from a DRC pass to a failure. This staged policy keeps the original portfolio behavior first, scales additional search breadth from the candidates' own budgets instead of a fixed iteration count, and never directly selects or forces a solver. ## Validation Dataset 18, effort 1x: | Sample | Completion | Relaxed DRC | High-density stage | | --- | --- | --- | ---: | | 1 | pass | fail, 2 errors (matches baseline) | 9.2s | | 2 | pass | fail, 72 errors | 105.5s | | 5 | pass | pass, 0 errors (matches baseline) | 10.8s | | 6 | pass | fail, 506 errors | 257.1s | - sample 2's butchered cross-board traces are visually removed - the captured hard node solves at its original size with 19 routes, without resize or invalid fallback output - 23 focused tests pass - production TypeScript and declaration build passes - formatting, lint, and diff checks pass

title: Add exact DRC branch portfolio
description: Adds a new GlobalDrcBranchPortfolioSolver to enhance DRC evaluation by allowing targeted error forces and broad repulsion strategies, improving the handling of local violations on dense boards.
body: tscircuit-autorouter results: ## Summary - keep the opt-in targeted exact-DRC sweep and post-solve relaxation control - add `GlobalDrcBranchPortfolioSolver` for callers that can provide an exact DRC evaluator - run the existing exact repair as the baseline, then generate a broad-repulsion candidate from the original input - refine the broad candidate only when its exact DRC count is already below the completed baseline - select the broad output only when its final exact DRC count is strictly lower - expose a separate iteration cap for the broad branch ## Why Targeted error forces are effective on local violations, but dense boards can be trapped in a poor geometric basin. Broad repulsion can escape that basin, yet applying it unconditionally regresses some boards. The portfolio makes the decision with the caller's exact DRC evaluator. The completed baseline is always retained unless the broad candidate has fewer exact violations, and the broad child solver only accepts non-worsening snapshots. Existing repair03 callers remain unchanged. ## Measured impact On five saved Pipeline7 pre-repair cases from dataset srj18, using the same parent global stage and exact evaluator: | Sample | Existing exact repair | Portfolio (8-iteration broad cap) | | --- | ---: | ---: | | 3 | 17 | 17 | | 7 | 7 | 4 | | 10 | 9 | 5 | | 11 | 9 | 2 | | 12 | 72 | 12 | | **Total** | **114** | **40** | Sample 3 is the important guard case: broad repulsion was worse, so the portfolio skipped that branch and retained the 17-error baseline. ## Validation - `bun test` — 25 passed - `bun run typecheck` - two focused portfolio tests cover accepting a lower-count broad branch and rejecting a worse broad candidate

The Pi session records provider failures in the final assistant message but resolves `session.prompt()`. `tsci-agent` treated that resolution as a successful run, printed `[agent] done`, and exited 0. Callers therefore saw an empty turn instead of a process failure.

The application declared `tsci-agent` as `latest`, but the committed lockfile still resolved 0.1.4. That release hides model request failures as empty successful turns and also sends the default `openai/gpt-5.6-sol` model through the incompatible Chat Completions protocol.

- GPT-5.6 Sol uses Pi's Responses API adapter

The Responses change is the client half of [aigateway.tscircuit.com#2](https://github.com/tscircuit/aigateway.tscircuit.com/pull/2) and was introduced by [tsci-agent#11](https://github.com/tscircuit/tsci-agent/pull/11).

title: Introduce a Secure Dockerized Runtime with Non-Root Execution and Peristent Job Storage
description: Adds a production-style Docker workflow for running the complete Datasheet-to-tscircuit application locally, including the web UI, API, tsci-agent, tsci, persistent job storage, authentication configuration, and PDF inspection tooling.
body:
## Summary

Adds a production-style Docker workflow for running the complete Datasheet-to-tscircuit application locally, including the web UI, API, `tsci-agent`, `tsci`, persistent job storage, authentication configuration, and PDF inspection tooling.

## Changes

* Added a multi-stage Bun Docker image for dependency installation, frontend builds, and runtime execution.
* Added Docker Compose configuration with loopback-only port exposure.
* Runs the application as a non-root user with all Linux capabilities dropped.
* Persists generated datasheets, TSX, Circuit JSON, and agent logs under `.runtime/jobs`.
* Added `.env.example` support for supplying `TSCIRCUIT_JWT` without committing credentials.
* Added `.dockerignore` rules to exclude secrets, runtime output, dependencies, and development files.
* Installed Poppler utilities for extracting datasheet text and rendering diagram pages.
* Updated agent workspace instructions to use `pdftotext`, `pdfinfo`, and `pdftoppm` before interpreting datasheets.
* Added configurable server hostname support with secure loopback binding by default.
* Added tests covering hostname resolution behavior.
* Expanded the README with Docker setup, persistence, security boundaries, and non-Docker development instructions.

## Impact

This provides a repeatable and safer local deployment workflow for the complete conversion stack while preserving generated job artifacts and protecting the service from accidental network exposure. It also improves datasheet analysis by giving the agent dedicated tools for inspecting text, pinout diagrams, and package drawings.

title: Fix .kicad_sym imports from static asset URLs
description: Fixes .kicad_sym imports when RunFrame or CLI represents the file as __STATIC_ASSET__ or a browser blob: URL.
body: ## Summary Fixes `.kicad_sym` imports when RunFrame or CLI represents the file as `__STATIC_ASSET__` or a browser `blob:` URL. Eval now fetches the actual file contents before passing them to `staticFileLoaderMap.kicad_sym`. ## Why is this needed? A `.kicad_sym` import must return Circuit JSON so it can be passed directly to the `symbol` prop: RunFrame can store static files in fsMap as __STATIC_ASSET__ instead of including their raw contents. It can also provide a blob: URL. Previously, the .kicad_sym loader received that marker or URL directly. However, KicadSymbolToCircuitJsonConverter requires the actual KiCad file contents, causing the import to fail with: This change resolves the marker or URL into an ArrayBuffer before invoking the static file loader. ## Why .kicad_mod did not need this ### .kicad_mod already exports a URL. Core later fetches that URL through footprintFileParserMap.kicad_mod.loadFromUrl(). ### .kicad_sym is different because it must be converted to Circuit JSON during import. Its loader therefore needs the actual file contents, not a URL. ## Testing ### Added a schematic snapshot test covering a .kicad_sym imported as __STATIC_ASSET__

title: Reduce post-route vias with safe geometry shortcuts
description: Reduces the number of vias in autorouting by optimizing layer transitions and applying geometric shortcuts, improving routing efficiency without introducing new global routing processes.
body:
## Summary

- make high-density segment conflict queries layer-aware while continuing to treat vias as cross-layer obstacles
- extend useless-via removal to replace `A → B → A` layer excursions with shorter or equal-length, clearance-checked 45-degree paths
- preserve jumper and through-obstacle geometry, respect the board outline, and refresh the route spatial index after every accepted optimization
- delay geometry shortcut search until the second simplification loop, after path simplification has reduced the candidate search space

## Why

The existing useless-via solver can only move the current geometry onto an adjacent layer. It leaves paired vias behind when that exact projected path is blocked, even when a shorter same-layer path is clear. Its shared segment conflict query also considered traces on unrelated copper layers, rejecting valid reductions.

This change reuses the existing trace simplification, connectivity, obstacle, and spatial-index checks to find safe geometric shortcuts without invoking another global router.

## Benchmark impact

Measured with Pipeline 7, effort 1, against the supplied srj18 benchmark report:

| Sample | Report baseline | This branch | Change |
| --- | ---: | ---: | ---: |
| srj18 #5 | 169 vias, 0 DRC | 159 vias, 0 DRC | -10 vias (-5.9%) |
| srj18 #12 | 442 vias, 12 DRC | 373 vias, 10 DRC | -69 vias (-15.6%) |

Relative to freshly stitched geometry, the full simplification pipeline reduces sample 5 from 219 to 159 vias and sample 12 from 594 to 373 vias.

## Validation

- `bun run build`
- focused useless-via, layer-filter, same-net merger, endpoint-layer, live-index, and simplification-overlap tests: 9 passing
- sample 5 relaxed DRC: 0 errors
- sample 12 relaxed DRC: 10 errors, down from 12 in the supplied report
- large bugreport66 fixture: 39.8 seconds, under its 60-second budget

The broad suite produces expected SVG snapshot differences because final trace geometry changes. Those snapshots were intentionally left unchanged for visual review.

description: Fixes routing issues for short direct traces around endpoint obstacles, ensuring accurate connections and improved trace generation.
body: ## Summary Fixes the missing `HOST.pin2 -> R1.pin1` route reproduced in #665. - classify explicit two-pin direct connections with the same Euclidean `schMaxTraceDistance` metric used when core creates the solver input - retain the existing Manhattan cutoff for named nets and multi-pin MST routing - generate symmetric, endpoint-anchored five-segment candidates when a three-point elbow's first or last segment hits an obstacle - add focused tests for both behaviors and update snapshots whose routing improves ## Root cause There were two independent failures: 1. Core measured this explicit source trace at ~1.83 using Euclidean distance, below the 2.4 cutoff, while the solver measured 2.55 using Manhattan distance and omitted the MSP pair. 2. Once admitted, the initial three-point elbow crossed C1. The existing recovery shifted the adjacent segment, but for a three-point elbow that also moves the opposite endpoint, so every candidate failed endpoint validation. ## Solver blast radius The full solver suite has seven intentional visual changes. No route is lost: examples 09/21/29 gain direct traces; existing bug reports recover failed or omitted routes; one large case preserves its route count while selecting collision-free same-net long-distance edges. ## Linked core validation Built and linked this branch into `tscircuit/core` with `bun link`, then ran all 264 test files containing `toMatchSchematicSnapshot`. - 283 pass, 5 skip - 6 SVG snapshot differences, all classified - repro46: 4 -> 5 real routes - repro91: adds `J1.2 -> U1.9` - repro129: adds `HOST.pin2 -> R1.1` - repro44: recovers the previously failed `R3.1 -> U1.3` route - example35: adds `J3.4 -> R16.1` and `J4.2 -> R19.1` - complex-crossings: adds four short resistor-to-U1 routes; one long cross-schematic line falls back to its existing net-label pair, preserving connectivity ## Test plan - `bun test` (119 pass, 4 skip, 0 fail) - `bunx tsc --noEmit` - focused repro and unit tests - linked core schematic-only blast-radius run described above Before and After:

title: Reproduce closed solder-jumper bridges missing from autorouter obstacles
description: Adds a regression test to demonstrate that the autorouter can incorrectly route a trace through a closed solder jumper bridge, highlighting a flaw in obstacle detection.
body: ## Summary Add a minimal regression case demonstrating that the autorouter can route an unrelated PCB trace through the fixed copper bridge of a closed solder jumper. ## Reproduction The fixture places: - a closed solder jumper with a rotated bridged footprint - two resistors on opposite sides of the jumper - an unrelated trace routed between the resistors The constrained 8 mm board and offset jumper make the failure deterministic. The autorouter treats the space between the jumper pads as available and routes through its copper bridge. PCB DRC subsequently reports an accidental contact between the bridge and the unrelated trace. ## Root cause evidence The closed bridge is emitted as a component-owned `pcb_trace`, but that fixed copper geometry is absent from the obstacle and existing-route data supplied to the autorouter. The router therefore sees the gap between the jumper pads as empty. ## Regression coverage The test verifies: - exactly one `pcb_trace_error` is produced - the error identifies the unrelated R1-to-R2 trace crossing JP1 - the collision is reported as accidental contact - the generated PCB geometry is captured in a snapshot ## Validation ```sh bun test tests/repros/repro149-autorouter-crosses-bridged-solderjumper.test.tsx bunx biome format tests/repros/repro149-autorouter-crosses-bridged-solderjumper.test.tsx ``` ## Scope This PR adds the focused reproduction and regression fixture only; it does not change production autorouting behavior.

title: Fix bridged solderjumper pads falsely flagged as trace-clearance violations by resolving port ids before placement DRC
description: Fixes clearance violations for bridged solderjumper pads by ensuring port ids are resolved before the design rule check, preventing autorouting issues.
body:
## Summary

Bridged solderjumpers (e.g. `<solderjumper footprint="solderjumper2_bridged12" bridgedPins={[["1","2"]]} />`) were incorrectly producing `pcb_pad_trace_clearance_error`s on their own pads, and this could cascade into skipped/blocked autorouting for traces connected through the jumper.

## Root cause

A bridged solderjumper footprint includes a physical bridge trace between its shorted pins, generated by the footprint library. Since real `pcb_port_id`s don't exist yet at footprint-generation time, that trace is emitted with placeholder endpoint ids: `"{PIN1}"`, `"{PIN2}"`.

`SolderJumper` and `Jumper` resolve these placeholders into real port ids inside `doInitialPcbTraceRender()`, which runs during the `PcbTraceRender` render phase (see `orderedRenderPhases` in `lib/components/base-components/Renderable.ts`). The issue is phase ordering: `PcbPlacementDesignRuleChecks` (pad/trace clearance checking) runs **one phase earlier** than `PcbTraceRender`.

So when the clearance checker builds its connectivity map, the bridge trace's endpoints are still the literal strings `"{PIN1}"`/`"{PIN2}"` — they don't resolve to any pad. The checker concludes the trace is unconnected to the two pads it's physically touching (by design, at 0 gap) and raises a clearance violation for each one. Since the codebase treats the diagnostic as authored by the DRC phase rather than a routing decision, this is unrelated to the autorouter itself — the connectivity is fine once the ids resolve, the check just runs too early.

## Fix

Renamed the placeholder-resolution method in both `SolderJumper` and `Jumper` from `doInitialPcbTraceRender()` to `doInitialPcbPortAttachment()`. This moves the same logic to the `PcbPortAttachment` phase, which runs immediately after ports are created (`PcbPortRender`) and well before `PcbPlacementDesignRuleChecks`. By the time clearance checks run, the bridge trace already references real port ids, so the connectivity map correctly treats the bridged pads as connected.

No behavior changes for non-bridged jumpers/solderjumpers — the fixup logic itself is untouched, only its render phase.

## Reproduction

Added `tests/repros/repro123-bridged-solderjumper-skips-autorouting.test.tsx`: two resistors wired through a bridged 2-pin solderjumper. Before this fix it produced 2 `pcb_pad_trace_clearance_error`s; after the fix it produces 0, and autorouting between the resistors succeeds.

## Testing

- [x] New repro test passes with 0 `pcb_pad_trace_clearance_error` / `pcb_autorouting_error`, snapshot updated
- [x] Existing solderjumper/jumper test suite (`solderjumper*.test.tsx`, `jumper*.test.tsx`, `pinrows*.test.tsx`, `auto-jumper-autorouting.test.tsx`, jumper-related repros) passes
- [x] Full test suite run (`bun test`): 1173 pass / 9 fail / 31 skip — the 9 failures are pre-existing timeouts/autorouter issues (differential-pair routing, pcb-pack layout) reproducible on unmodified `main` and unrelated to this change

title: deduplicate Core PCB DRC errors
description: Deduplicates PCB DRC errors in Cores circuit JSON by applying a deduplication function at the DRC aggregation boundary, ensuring only specific clearance diagnostics are reported while removing legacy overlap records.
body:
## Summary

- apply `dedupePcbDrcErrors` once at Core's DRC aggregation boundary
- bump `@tscircuit/checks` to `0.0.144`, the first release exporting the shared deduper
- keep typed pad/trace clearance diagnostics while removing matching legacy `pcb_trace_error` overlap records
- update breakout assertions to target the typed clearance diagnostics

## Root cause

Core inserted the flattened results of routing, placement, netlist, pin-specification, and custom DRC checks directly into circuit JSON. When one check emitted a typed pad/trace or via/trace clearance error and another emitted the legacy generic overlap record for the same pair, both survived and rendered as duplicate markers and labels.

## Impact

Consumers of Core circuit JSON now receive one specific clearance diagnostic for each affected pad/trace or via/trace pair. Unrelated generic trace diagnostics, such as disconnected endpoints, are preserved.

## Validation

- `bunx tsc --noEmit`
- `bun run build`
- focused DRC regression and three affected breakout tests pass
- full suite completed with 1,176 passing and 31 skipped; its only unrelated remaining failure after updating the three dedupe-sensitive assertions is the differential-pair `port-selector` PCB snapshot, which also fails unchanged on a clean `origin/main` worktree with `@tscircuit/checks@0.0.143`

title: Classify trace-obstacle DRC errors at the source
description: Classifies trace-to-padvia gaps through one shared geometry path, ensuring that overlapping segments suppress clearance reports from other segments of the same trace, and places typed trace-clearance diagnostics at the center of their full trace route.
body:
## Summary

- compute trace-to-pad/via gaps through one shared geometry path
- emit `pcb_trace_error` only for contact/overlap (`gap <= 0`)
- emit typed pad/via clearance errors only for positive gaps below the required clearance
- classify across the whole trace-obstacle pair, so an overlapping segment suppresses clearance reports from other segments of the same trace
- place typed trace-clearance diagnostics at the center of their full trace route, making circuit-json the source of truth for distinct error placement
- run pad-trace clearance with routing checks and remove the runners' dependency on post-hoc deduplication

## Why

The same trace-pad/via pair could previously be emitted as both a generic overlap record and a typed clearance record. Deduplication hid the double report but could retain a clearance record with a negative `actual_clearance`, which mislabeled a real overlap.

This change makes the checkers mutually exclusive at the source: every physical pair is reported once with the correct semantic category. The shared geometry path still measures the closest trace and obstacle copper edges for classification, while each typed clearance record places `error.center` at the midpoint of its full trace route. Separate traces near the same obstacle therefore retain distinct source-defined placements without renderer-side label relocation.

## Validation

- `bun test` — 130 passed
- `bunx tsc --noEmit`
- `bun run build`
- Biome check on all changed TypeScript files
- focused center coverage for rectangular, circular, pill-pad, and via obstacles
- exact SOT-23 repro assertion that both typed error centers remain distinct
- regenerated and visually reviewed the affected circuit-to-SVG snapshots
- installed the local checks build into `tscircuit/tscircuit-autorouter` latest main (`v0.0.682`)
  - Dataset 18 sample 9: 28 DRCs, matching the earlier deduped result
  - identical-output comparison: old dedupe = 28 physical keys, new classification = 28 physical keys, 0 missing, 0 added
  - `bugreport-b5b3b9d8` remains at 6 DRCs with the existing type breakdown

The sample 9 record types now reflect reality: three pad overlaps and one via overlap move from typed clearance records to overlap records, while the physical issue set and total remain unchanged.

title: Add long-running PR benchmark option
description: Add a new command benchmark-long to allow long-running PR benchmarks with an increased timeout and worker configuration.
body:
## Summary

- increase the regular autorouting benchmark timeout from 60 to 120 minutes
- add `/benchmark-long [benchmark.sh args...]` for long-running PR benchmarks
- run long benchmarks on `blacksmith-8vcpu-ubuntu-2404-arm` with 8 workers by default and a 480-minute timeout
- document the new command in PR benchmark instructions

## Why

PR comment benchmarks currently share one runner configuration and a one-hour timeout. Larger datasets such as srj18 can exhaust that window while using only four workers on the configured runner. The new command provides an explicit higher-capacity option without making every benchmark an eight-hour job.

`/benchmark-long` accepts the same arguments as `/benchmark`; for example:

```text
/benchmark-long --dataset srj18
```

Callers can still override `--concurrency` when needed.

## Validation

title: [-182 vias in dataset 18] Reduce multilayer vias by collapsing adjacent route sections
description: Reduces the number of multilayer vias by collapsing adjacent route sections when the geometry allows, optimizing the routing process.
body:
## What changed

- Collapse an intermediate route section in `A → B → C` layer patterns onto either adjacent layer when the geometry remains clear.
- Reuse the live high-density route index, connectivity map, obstacle index, and conservative trace/pad margins for every candidate.
- Preserve jumpers, jumper-pad geometry, and special through-obstacle transitions.
- Merge collapsed sections immediately so later passes can safely cascade and remove additional vias.
- Add focused success and collision-regression tests.

## Why

The existing useless-via solver handled `A → B → A` excursions but skipped middle sections when the outer layers differed. On multilayer boards this left many redundant intermediate layer hops, including zero-length `A → B → C` transitions at one coordinate. A normal through via can connect the remaining layers directly, so the intermediate section and one via are unnecessary whenever that section's geometry is also legal on an adjacent layer.

## Benchmark impact

Sequential SRJ18 Pipeline 7 runs on current `main` (`v0.0.685`):

| Sample | Merged main | This PR | Removed | Reduction |
| --- | ---: | ---: | ---: | ---: |
| 12 | 373 | 320 | 53 | 14.2% |
| 14 | 318 | 294 | 24 | 7.5% |
| 5 (two-layer control) | 159 | 159 | 0 | unchanged |

For sample 12, a controlled run with only this collapse disabled took 15.29 s in trace simplification; enabled took 13.88 s. The added pass did not measurably increase simplification time.

Sample 12 has 10 pre-existing relaxed-DRC issues on merged main. The PR produces the identical breakdown: 4 trace errors, 4 pad/trace clearance errors, and 2 via-clearance errors.

## Validation

- `bun test tests/solvers/useless-via-removal-*.test.ts tests/bugs/keyboard4-pipeline5-live-cmn132-layer-change-connector.test.ts --timeout 9999999` — 8 passed, 1 pre-existing skip
- `bun run build`
- Sequential benchmark and relaxed-DRC comparison described above

title: Fix packing obstacles for rotated SMT pads
description: Fixes the issue where rotated SMT pads were skipped during packing, leading to potential component collisions and invalid packing obstacles.
body:
## Summary

Rotated pads are converted into conservative axis-aligned bounding boxes using their `width`, `height`, and `ccw_rotation`.

## Why is this needed?

Previously, rotated SMT pads were silently skipped by `extractPadInfos`.

This could cause:

- missing component collision geometry
- physical overlap between packed components
- missing pad/network information during placement optimization
- components containing only rotated pads to produce invalid (`NaN`) centers
- fixed components containing only rotated pads to be omitted from packing obstacles

Real Circuit JSON fixtures already contain many `rotated_rect` pads, so this is required for correct general Circuit JSON conversion even though some fixtures only use normal rectangular pads.

Silkscreen elements are intentionally not treated as packing obstacles. Silkscreen is printed artwork rather than physical component clearance geometry; courtyards, pads, component bounds, keepouts, and board cutouts should define packing clearance.

## Changes

- Added rotation-aware AABB calculation for rotated SMT pads.
- Added support for `rotated_rect` and `rotated_pill`.
- Normalized floating-point noise for orthogonal rotations.
- Added regression tests for:
  - 90° rotated rectangles
  - 90° rotated pills
  - fixed components containing only rotated pads
  - finite obstacle centers
  - conservative AABB dimensions at 45°
- Updated the existing Circuit JSON visual snapshot to include previously skipped rotated pads.

title: Fix fixed-component packing obstacles to respect PCB courtyards
description: Fixes packing obstacle generation for fixed PCB components by using their courtyard bounds instead of only their pad bounds, preventing auto-placed components from being packed inside reserved component-clearance areas.
body:
## Summary

Fix packing obstacle generation for fixed PCB components by using their courtyard bounds instead of only their pad bounds.

Fixed components using `position_mode: "relative_to_group_anchor"` previously produced obstacles based solely on their pads. This allowed automatically packed components to enter the larger courtyard area and caused courtyard overlap errors even though the packing result appeared valid.

## Root cause

The circuit JSON conversion path handled courtyard geometry for packed components, but fixed relative components always fell back to pad-derived bounds. Components such as connectors and ICs can have courtyards that extend well beyond their pads, so those obstacles understated the reserved PCB area.

## Changes

- Extract courtyard geometry for fixed components during circuit JSON conversion
- Generate an accurately positioned obstacle from the courtyard dimensions and offset
- Preserve pad-bound obstacle generation as a fallback for components without courtyards
- Avoid generating duplicate pad and courtyard obstacles for the same fixed component

## Bug reproduction

Added a regression test with:

- A fixed component using relative positioning
- A small SMT pad
- A larger, offset courtyard outline

The test verifies that the resulting packing obstacle uses the courtyard's full dimensions and absolute center rather than the smaller pad bounds.

## Impact

This improves packing obstacle accuracy for connectors, ICs, and other fixed components with courtyards larger than their pads. It prevents auto-placed components from being packed inside reserved component-clearance areas.

The issue was reproduced on the SparkFun RS232 Breakout, where capacitors could be placed inside the courtyards of the fixed transceiver and DB9 connector.

## Validation

- 109 tests passed
- 10 tests skipped
- Typecheck passed
- Build passed
- Formatting checks passed
- Verified against the SparkFun RS232 Breakout circuit

title: Align parallel passives sharing a common pin
description: Fixes placement of parallel passives sharing a common pin to ensure correct alignment in the layout.
body:
Fixes the placement captured in #159.

## Root cause

The generic packer only minimizes pad distance. For repro70, the rotated capacitor bodies are slightly cheaper to stack vertically, so it produces a column even though C1/C3 form parallel rails.

`ParallelAlignedPassiveSolver` already owns this semantic placement, but its detector only recognized groups of at least three passives directly attached to a main chip. It did not recognize two or more passives sharing one strong-connected hub pin.

## Fix

Extend `findSameSidePassiveGroups` to recognize a common-pin fanout when:

- at least two two-pin leaf components share the hub;
- their opposite pins share the same net;
- their opposite pins are not strongly anchored elsewhere; and
- exactly one pin remains as the unambiguous anchor.

The existing `ParallelAlignedPassiveSolver` then places the group using its normal pin-aware reflow. There are no component IDs, coordinates, distance overrides, or repro-specific cases. `AlignPowerGroundRowsSolver` is unchanged.

## Verification

- Full suite: 46 pass, 1 skip, 0 fail
- Existing BQ24074 same-side-passive snapshots unchanged
- Build passes
- Biome format check passes
- Snapshot blast radius: repro70 only

title: test: reproduce core repro70 common-node placement
description: Reproduces the incorrect placement of a common node in the layout solver, capturing the behavior for debugging without changing matchpack behavior.
body:
## Summary

- capture the exact matchpack `InputProblem` emitted by `@tscircuit/core` for `repro70-schematicbox-rotation-autolayout`
- record that the common component `C1` is placed outside its two strongly connected neighbors, `FB1` and `C3`
- add a semantic assertion and SVG snapshot for the bad placement

This PR contains repro coverage only and does not change matchpack behavior.

## Reproduced behavior

`C1.1` is the common pin for the strong connections `FB1.2-C1.1` and `C1.1-C3.1`. With the `calculate-packing@0.0.78` version now on main, matchpack places both neighbors on the same side of `C1`:

- `C1`: `(0, 0)`
- `C3`: approximately `(0, 1.3)`
- `FB1`: approximately `(-1, 2.85)`

The direction dot product from `C1` to those neighbors is `3.705` (positive), which semantically confirms that `C1` is outside rather than between them. This makes the `FB1-C1` connection unnecessarily long and reproduces the placement seen in core.

## Validation

title: Render JSCAD CAD model plans
description: Render cad_component.model_jscad as a normal scene mesh, execute serialized plans with jscad-planner, convert the resulting Z-up JSCAD geometry into the renderers Y-up coordinate system, and visually render an open-top JSCAD enclosure with representative SOIC-8, QFP-32, and DIP-8 models from jscad-electronics.
body:
## Summary

- render `cad_component.model_jscad` as a normal scene mesh
- execute serialized plans with `jscad-planner`
- convert the resulting Z-up JSCAD geometry into the renderer's Y-up coordinate system
- cover direct plan loading, Circuit JSON scene conversion, and the complete GLTF/PoppyGL visual pipeline
- visually render an open-top JSCAD enclosure with representative SOIC-8, QFP-32, and DIP-8 models from `jscad-electronics`

## Why

The initial enclosure implementation in `tscircuit/core` emits serializable JSCAD plans, following [addibble/pcb-enclosure](https://github.com/addibble/pcb-enclosure). `circuit-json-to-gltf` currently ignores CAD components whose only model source is `model_jscad`, so those enclosures do not reach the existing GLTF/PoppyGL pipeline.

This is intentionally generic renderer support: it contains no enclosure-specific geometry or behavior.

Related props API: [tscircuit/props#732](https://github.com/tscircuit/props/pull/732)

## Validation

- `bun --config=/dev/null test tests/unit/jscad-plan.test.ts`
- generated and visually inspected `jscad-plan-with-electronics.snap.png`
- `bunx tsc --noEmit`
- `bun run build`
- `bun run format:check`
- `git diff --check`

The local checkout's native `sharp` binding is incomplete, so final PNG comparison is delegated to GitHub CI. The full render pipeline completed locally to generate the committed baseline; GitHub CI exercises the normal matcher preload and cross-platform comparison.

title: Fix U-shaped endpoint obstacle detours
description: Fixes the endpoint collision detour generation for U-shaped base elbows to ensure proper routing around obstacles while preserving the U bend and endpoint-facing approach.
body:
## Summary

- extend the existing endpoint-collision detour generator from L-shaped base elbows to fixed-pair U-shaped base elbows
- preserve the untouched remainder of the U path so the required endpoint-facing approach is not lost
- keep optional long-distance routing and endpoint-chip routing on their established strategies because those require net-level or multi-trace selection
- update repro47 and the equivalent example11 snapshots from label fallback to collision-free traces

## Root cause

Same-facing pins produce a four-point U-shaped base elbow. When an intermediate component blocked the first or last segment, the solver used its generic adjacent-segment shift. That shift cannot insert the extra dogleg needed to go around the obstacle while preserving the U bend and the pin-facing approach, so the candidate search exhausted and the pair fell back to net labels. The existing endpoint-detour helper already models the correct operation, but only retained three-point L paths.

This change generalizes that helper to retain the suffix/prefix of four-point U paths. It does not add circuit-specific coordinates, distance exceptions, or iteration increases.

Repro introduced in #667.

## Validation

- `bun test` — 122 pass, 4 skip, 0 fail
- `bun run build`
- `bun run format:check`
- generated and inspected every pipeline stage for repro47
- reviewed every changed snapshot; final blast radius is only repro47 and equivalent example11, and both replace the same two failed pairs with real obstacle-clearing traces
- explicitly rejected broader variants that regressed example40 or reduced real traces in bug-report-20260706T220324Z

title: Fix routing past open obstacle sides
description: Fixes routing failure when generating candidates for paths that encounter open obstacle sides, allowing for proper routing around obstacles in schematic traces.
body:
## Summary

- let fixed MSP routes generate a standard-clearance candidate beyond an obstacle when that side has no neighboring bound
- retain the existing bounded search for optional long-distance candidates
- route repro5 around the combined U1 and padded-text obstacle region instead of falling back to labels
- add focused coverage for bounded versus open-side candidate generation

## Root cause

Repro5 first moved the route above or below U1 correctly. That candidate then collided with a padded reference/part-number text box. `candidateMidsFromSet` only generated coordinates halfway toward an existing neighbor. Since there was no neighbor on the exterior side of the text box, it never generated an exterior candidate; its only candidate pointed back into U1, and the queue exhausted.

The fix uses the same `0.2` obstacle clearance already used by `midBetweenPointAndRect` when the fixed route has an open side. It does not alter `maxMspPairDistance`, add circuit coordinates, or raise iteration limits.

Repro introduced in #667.

## Validation

- `bun test` — 122 pass, 4 skip, 0 fail
- `bun run build`
- `bun run format:check`
- generated and inspected all 16 repro5 pipeline stages; the trace clears U1 and both padded text obstacles
- reviewed the complete snapshot blast radius; final change is repro5 only
- explicitly rejected an unbounded long-distance variant after it produced a 23.5-unit loop in bug-report-20260707T140410Z; optional candidates remain bounded and both unrelated snapshots stay unchanged

title: fix KiCad schematic passive symbols and net label connections
description: Fixes KiCad schematic passive symbols and net label connections by addressing duplicate lead lines and correcting misaligned power-symbol instances.
body:
fixes: https://github.com/tscircuit/circuit-json-to-kicad/pull/377

Resistor symbols exported to KiCad were drawing duplicate lead lines because the imported symbol artwork already contained lead stubs and KiCad pins added another visible line on top.

GND and V3_3 symbols were not truly connected because the power-symbol instance was placed by symbol origin, while the actual KiCad pin was offset from that origin, so the wire endpoint missed the real connection point.

title: Prefer relaxed electrical meander geometry
description: Generates relaxed, intermediate, and minimum-clearance pitch candidates for free-space routing, ranking geometry with electrical-risk proxies and preserving compact routing around obstacles.
body:
## Summary

- generate relaxed, intermediate, and minimum-clearance pitch candidates so free-space routing prefers broad shallow meanders
- rank geometry with stackup-independent electrical-risk proxies and compare single- versus multi-segment plans without rewarding fragmentation
- retain pitch alternatives through multi-segment allocation, center the real lobe footprint, and preserve compact routing around obstacles
- document the research basis, limitations, ownership map, tuning inputs, and focused tests for users and AI agents

## Research basis

The design follows AMD and TI guidance to avoid dense self-coupled serpentines where possible, plus published meander-alleviation research favoring wider, more evenly distributed tuning. The score remains explicitly documented as a geometry heuristic rather than calculated impedance, inductance, EMI, or delay.

## Code review fixes

- preserve relaxed pitch choices instead of collapsing each segment to maximum-capacity compact geometry
- allow materially gentler distributed plans to beat a deep single-segment solution with an explicit per-segment cost
- center meanders using their actual occupied footprint
- penalize deviations from intended tapered or uniform profiles and keep a marginal cost for every active tooth
- include all current geometry-defining fields in planned-attempt identity

## Validation

- bun run format:check
- bunx tsc --noEmit
- bun test --timeout 9999999 (18 pass)
- bun run build
- git diff --check
- refreshed SVG snapshot and inspected a generated 1536x1536 PNG artifact
